title: Farrowing sow management and piglet care
subtitle: Attending farrowing, managing the sow, and giving newborn piglets the best start.
excerpt: Farrowing is the highest-risk point in a pig's life. Most piglet deaths happen in the first 48 hours, the majority from crushing by the sow. Being present at farrowing, knowing when to intervene, and acting quickly on weak piglets determines how many you raise. This tutorial covers the signs of approaching farrowing, what to do during the birth, and the key decisions in the first two days.
sourceNotes:
AHDB Pork, Improving piglet survival. https://ahdb.org.uk
DEFRA, Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Livestock: Pigs (2003). https://www.gov.uk
Nadis, Farrowing and lactation management. https://www.nadis.org.uk
Pig Veterinary Society, Iron deficiency and colostrum management guidance. https://www.pigvetsoc.org.uk

glossaryTerms.definition: The act of a sow giving birth. The farrowing process typically takes two to six hours for the full litter, with each piglet arriving at intervals of 15 to 30 minutes. A pause of more than 45 minutes between piglets during active labour may indicate an obstruction requiring veterinary attention.

The first milk produced by the sow immediately after farrowing, rich in antibodies (immunoglobulins) that give piglets passive immunity against disease. Piglets can only absorb immunoglobulins from colostrum in the first 12 to 24 hours of life. A piglet that does not receive colostrum in this window has a greatly reduced chance of survival.

An intramuscular injection of iron dextran given to piglets within 24 to 48 hours of birth. Piglets are born with very limited iron stores and sow's milk provides little iron. Without supplemental iron, piglets develop anaemia at two to three weeks and grow poorly. The injection is given in the neck muscle using a small-gauge needle.

A horizontal bar fixed to the inside wall of the farrowing pen, roughly 20 to 25 cm above the floor and 20 cm out from the wall. The rail creates a space where piglets can shelter when the sow lies down, preventing them from being trapped and crushed between the sow and the wall. Widely regarded as the single most effective low-cost intervention for reducing crush deaths.

Surgical removal of part of the tail, performed to reduce tail-biting in intensive systems. In the UK, tail docking is not permitted as a routine procedure and is only lawful when there is evidence of tail-biting occurring and enrichment provision has been addressed. For small outdoor herds, tail docking is rarely appropriate.

content.text: A sow's gestation is 114 days (three months, three weeks, three days is the mnemonic). Mark the expected
farrowing
date from the service date and begin checking the sow closely from day 110. She will tell you farrowing is close.
Signs that farrowing is near

The vulva swells and softens two to three days before farrowing. The udder fills and becomes tight in the 24 hours before. Milk can be expressed from teats in the last few hours. The sow becomes restless, nesting repeatedly, pawing at bedding and circling. Most sows farrow in the early hours of the morning.
Setting up the farrowing area
A deep straw bed in a clean pen is the starting point. Fix a
farrowing rail
around the inside walls before the sow enters the pen. Set up a
heat lamp

in one corner to create a warm creep area. Newborn piglets need 32 to 34 degrees Celsius; the sow prefers considerably cooler. The creep corner separates these two environments without a physical barrier.
Attending the birth

Stay present but quiet. Each piglet is born in its membranes. Clear the membrane from the face immediately if the sow does not do it herself. Rub the piglet briskly with a clean towel to stimulate breathing. Once breathing, guide the piglet to a teat and confirm it sucks. The first feeds of
colostrum
must happen within the first two hours of life.

Watch for the sow standing between piglet arrivals. When she stands, move piglets away from her feet and into the creep corner. Return them to her when she lies back down. The crush hazard is highest in the first six to twelve hours before the sow settles.

attrs.title: When to call the vet
attrs.body: Call your vet if no piglet has appeared after 30 minutes of strong straining. Also call if straining has stopped for 45 minutes with more piglets still inside. Call if a piglet appears stuck or the sow is very distressed. Do not try to help internally unless trained to do so.

content.text: Iron injection for piglets
Give an
iron injection

to each piglet within 24 to 48 hours of birth. The standard dose is 200 mg of iron dextran injected into the neck muscle. Use a fresh needle for each piglet. Keep piglets in a warm box during the procedure and return them to the sow immediately. Piglets that do not receive iron develop anaemia at two to three weeks, becoming pale, lethargic, and prone to scouring.
Tail docking and teeth clipping decisions
UK law does not permit routine
tail docking

. It is only lawful when tail-biting has been observed and you have documented efforts to address it through enrichment and management. For small outdoor herds with adequate space and enrichment, tail docking is not appropriate and should not be performed.

Teeth clipping (removing the needle teeth) is similarly restricted. Piglets have eight sharp needle teeth that can injure the sow's udder or other piglets' faces. For small litters on a well-nourished sow, needle teeth rarely cause a problem that requires intervention. If the sow is rejecting the litter due to teat pain, veterinary advice should be obtained before clipping.
